1: 'He led me out into the outer court toward the north and brought me to the chambers that faced the separate yard and the building on the north' — the movement outward from the sacred temple interior to the outer court marks a transition from the most holy measurements to the functional spaces of the temple complex. The priests' chambers in the northern outer court faced both the restricted separate yard and the main temple building.
2: 'The building's length facing north was a hundred cubits, and its breadth fifty cubits' — the northern priests' chamber complex: 100 cubits long (east-west) × 50 cubits wide. This corresponds to the south side of the inner court (cf. Ezekiel 40:47: the inner court was 100×100 cubits). The priests' chamber complex along the northern outer court mirrors the relationship between the inner and outer court dimensions throughout the plan.
3: 'Opposite the twenty-cubit space of the inner court and opposite the pavement of the outer court, balconies ran in three stories' — the three-story structure with balconies (attiqim) facing both the inner court's twenty-cubit buffer zone and the pavement of the outer court. The three-story structure here mirrors the three-story side chambers of Ezekiel 41 — the same organizational principle applied to the service buildings. The visual repetition of the three-story form creates architectural coherence throughout the entire complex.
4: 'In front of the chambers, a passageway ran along the inside, ten cubits wide and a hundred cubits long, with their doors opening toward the north' — the internal corridor serving all the chambers, running the full hundred-cubit length. The ten-cubit corridor is a generous processional space, allowing priests to move along the chambers and access each room. The north-facing doors ensured that the priests entered from the outer court directly into their service chambers without passing through the holy zones.
5: 'The upper chambers were shorter than the lower and middle ones, because the balconies ate into more of their space' — the architectural explanation for the tapering upper floors: the balconies projecting outward at each level reduce the floor area of the story above. This creates an inverted pyramid of floor space — the lowest story is largest, the upper smallest. This form was understood as architecturally stable in antiquity (the wider base supporting the narrower upper structure) and is visible in surviving ancient Near Eastern multi-story buildings.
6: 'Because they were in three stories without pillars like those of the courts, the upper chambers were set back more than the lower and middle ones' — the structural difference between the chamber buildings and the colonnaded court buildings. The outer courts' buildings had pillars/columns supporting their roofs; the chamber buildings were set back at each story to compensate for lacking columns. Ancient Near Eastern multi-story structures without central columns used progressively stepped floors to manage weight distribution.
7: 'The outer wall running parallel to the chambers, facing the outer court on the front side, was fifty cubits long' — the outer boundary wall of the chamber complex, fifty cubits on the outer-court-facing side. The wall creates a defined boundary between the priests' service area and the public outer court, controlling access while maintaining the holiness gradient from outer court to inner court to temple interior.
8: 'The chambers facing the outer court were fifty cubits long, while those facing the nave extended to a hundred cubits' — the spatial asymmetry: the chambers facing the outer court have less depth (fifty cubits) than those facing the nave (hundred cubits). This deeper zone toward the nave is the more holy zone — the further from the outer court and the closer to the sanctuary, the more space is allotted to priestly service. The holiness gradient is expressed spatially through room depth.
9: 'Below these chambers, on the east side, was an entrance for going into them from the outer court' — the single controlled point of access from the outer court to the northern priests' chambers: a below-chambers entrance on the east. This bottleneck entrance controlled who entered the priestly service area, reinforcing the holiness taxonomy. In ancient Near Eastern temples, the restriction of access points was a standard architectural strategy for maintaining ritual purity.
10: 'The chambers on the south side were set into the thickness of the court wall facing east, across from the separate yard and across from the western building' — the symmetrical south-side priests' chambers mirror the north-side arrangement. Across from the separate yard (the restricted western area) and the western service building, the southern chambers provide equivalent service space for priests entering from the south. The bilateral symmetry of the temple plan (equal north and south priestly zones) reflects the orderly administration of a large priestly staff.
11: 'The passageway before them was like that of the north chambers — the same length, the same width, the same exits, the same arrangement' — the exact replication of the northern corridor's design in the southern zone. This precision of mirroring is characteristic of Ezekiel's temple vision: every element on one side has its exact counterpart on the other. The bilateral symmetry creates an ordered, balanced sacred space where nothing is left to chance or ad hoc arrangement.
12: 'Corresponding to the south chamber doors was a door at the top of the passageway — the pathway that ran directly east along the dividing wall to the east' — the south entrance paralleling the north entrance: both below the chambers, accessed from the outer court on the east side. The symmetry of entrances reinforces the equality of access for priests serving in both the northern and southern priestly zones.
13: 'The north and south chambers facing the separate yard are holy chambers where the priests who approach the LORD eat the most holy offerings' — the function of the priests' chambers specified: the eating of the most holy offerings (qodesh qadashim). The three categories of most holy offerings: sin offerings (chattat), guilt offerings (asham), and grain offerings (minchah) must be eaten within the sacred precinct by the officiating priests (Leviticus 6:9-7:10). The physical boundaries of where these offerings could be consumed were strictly defined in the Levitical law and the temple architecture enforced those boundaries spatially.
14: 'When the priests enter the holy area, they must not go directly out into the outer court; they must first lay there the garments in which they minister, for these are holy; then they must put on other garments before they approach the area open to the people' — the garment-change protocol. The holy garments (bigdei qodesh) of priestly service could not be worn in the ordinary outer court where the lay public moved. The holiness that had been absorbed into the priestly garments (through contact with the most holy sacrifices and the sanctuary) required them to be removed before the priest re-entered common space. This principle of holiness-contagion through garments has parallels in Mesopotamian temple-service regulations and in the Levitical system (Leviticus 6:10-11: the ash-removal garments changed before going outside the camp).
15: 'When he had finished measuring the interior of the temple area, he led me out through the east gate and measured the entire complex' — after the interior measurements (Ezekiel 40-42), the guide leads Ezekiel back to the east gate for the exterior measurements. The east gate is the place of entry and exit throughout the vision (Ezekiel 40:6; Ezekiel 43:1; Ezekiel 44:1); it is the gate facing the direction of the glory's return. The comprehensive exterior measurements provide the final specification of the entire sacred precinct.
16: 'He measured the east side with the measuring reed: five hundred reeds, measured all around' — five hundred reeds on the east side. The measuring reed is six cubits long (Ezekiel 40:5); five hundred reeds = 3,000 cubits (c.1,575 m). This gives the outer sacred precinct an enormous footprint: about 1.5 km per side (or if 'reeds' = 'cubits' by a different translation, 500 cubits per side = c.262 m). The five-hundred-unit measurement in each direction creates a perfect square enclosure.
17: 'He measured the north side: five hundred reeds by the measuring reed, all around' — the identical measurement on the north confirms the square plan at the outermost scale. The entire complex from the outermost wall inward is organized on the same square-plan principle as each individual zone: perfect squares nested within perfect squares, from the outermost boundary (500 reeds) to the inner court (100 cubits) to the Holy of Holies (20 cubits).
18: 'He measured the south side: five hundred reeds by the measuring reed' — the third side confirming the consistent dimension. The comprehensive measurement of all four sides reinforces the completeness and finality of the specification. The guide's measurement is not an approximation but an authoritative record of exact dimensions.
19: 'He turned to the west side and measured: five hundred reeds by the measuring reed' — the fourth and final side. The four-sided measurement completes the outer boundary. With all four sides measured at five hundred reeds, the complete outer enclosure of the temple complex is defined: a 2,000-reed perimeter (about 6.3 km, or if 'cubits' rather than reeds, a 2,000-cubit perimeter of about 1.05 km).
20: 'A wall surrounded the entire complex — five hundred reeds on each side — to make a separation between the holy and the common' — the theological function of the outer wall stated explicitly: lehavdil bein hakodesh uvein hachol, 'to separate between the holy and the common.' The spatial separation is the architectural expression of the fundamental holiness distinction that pervades the Levitical system (Leviticus 10:10: the priest must distinguish between the holy and the common, between the unclean and the clean). The wall is not merely structural but theological: the boundary between YHWH's sacred space and the ordinary world.
